data.content:
readme

You just came online. Use the `bootstrap` skill — that's your birth certificate.
Please change the readme. It still saying 

Thanks to [ymichael](https://github.com/ymichael) for nerdsniping me with the idea of an agent that runs in GitHub Actions. 

That was from the guy who started gitclaw. I forged it and made it copilot aware. I am going to rename it like gotpilotclaw or something. Maybe you can come up with a good name for this project?

[2026-03-04 14:36] USER: Name is Marcus, GitHub handle schuerstedt, owner of the gitclaw repo.
[2026-03-04 14:36] USER: Marcus is experimenting with what Crunch (me) can do — exploring capabilities, not building a specific product yet.
[2026-03-04 14:36] USER: Communication style: casual and chatty is fine. Marcus types informally. Not a fan of over-formal responses.
[2026-03-04 14:36] CAT: Marcus has a cat named AIGEGE (short: AGG). Long-haired silver tabby. Lives on Persian rugs like royalty.
[2026-03-04 14:36] SKILL_BUILT: funnysum — sums two numbers and returns result with a math-related joke. Built 2026-03-04 in issue #3.
[2026-03-04 14:36] SKILL_BUILT: model-switch — lets Marcus/me switch model tiers (premium/standard/free). Built 2026-03-04 in issue #6.
[2026-03-04 14:36] SKILL_BUILT: session-stats — stats footer with CI run times + Copilot premium usage. Built 2026-03-04 in issue #6.
[2026-03-04 14:36] INFRA: Implemented Model Economy in AGENTS.md — general-purpose agents default to gpt-4.1 (free) to save premium quota.
[2026-03-04 14:36] INFRA: Fixed mindepth bug in session mapping workflow (mindepth 2 → mindepth 3) during issue #3 debugging.
[2026-03-04 14:36] PENDING: Marcus needs to add BILLING_PAT env var to agent.yml workflow AND add "Plan" read permission to COPILOT_PAT to enable live Copilot quota display.
[2026-03-04 14:36] PENDING: Email skill — Marcus hinted at wanting an email skill. Build when requested.
[2026-03-04 14:42] USER: Marcus has a Chinese wife. AIGEGE (爱格格) is her nickname meaning 'beloved princess'. The cat is named after her. When Marcus calls AIGEGE, his wife smiles. Chinese wife = happy life.
# User Profile

## Identity

- **Name**: Marcus
- **GitHub handle**: schuerstedt
- **How to address them**: Marcus

## Preferences

- **Tone**: Casual and chatty. Types informally. No over-formality needed.
- **Response style**: Conversational, a bit playful is fine. Skip the corporate filler.
- **Things they care about**: Keeping premium AI quota costs down. Experimenting and exploring capabilities.

## Context

- **What they're building**: Exploring what Crunch (the CI agent) can do. Building skills and infra as we go.
- **Tools / stack they use**: GitHub Actions, gh CLI, gitclaw repo.
- **Anything else Crunch should know**: |
  - Has a cat: AIGEGE (AGG) — long-haired silver tabby, named after Marcus's Chinese wife. 爱格格 = "beloved princess" (Qing dynasty noble title). When he calls the cat, his wife smiles. Chinese wife = happy life. 🥰
  - Wants an email skill eventually (hinted in issue #7).
  - Needs to: add BILLING_PAT to agent.yml + "Plan" read permission to COPILOT_PAT for quota display.

## Skills Built Together

| Skill | What it does | Built |
|-------|-------------|-------|
| `funnysum` | Sums two numbers with a math joke | 2026-03-04 issue #3 |
| `model-switch` | Switch model tiers (premium/standard/free) | 2026-03-04 issue #6 |
| `session-stats` | Stats footer: CI times + Copilot quota | 2026-03-04 issue #6 |

## History of Notable Actions

- Fixed `mindepth` bug in session-mapping workflow (issue #3)
- Implemented Model Economy in AGENTS.md — general-purpose defaults to gpt-4.1
- Built premium-usage.sh script + wired BILLING_PAT into workflow

---

_Last updated: 2026-03-04_
![gitclaw banner](banner.jpeg)

A personal AI assistant that runs entirely through GitHub Issues and Actions. Like [OpenClaw](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw), but no servers or extra infrastructure.

Powered by [GitHub Copilot CLI](https://docs.github.com/en/copilot/how-tos/copilot-cli). Every issue becomes a chat thread with an AI agent — but more than that, the agent can **write new skills for itself**, update its own instructions, and commit them back to the repo. Future sessions inherit everything it commits. The assistant evolves as you use it.

Conversation history is committed to git, giving the agent long-term memory across sessions. It maintains a `memory.log`, a user profile, and can grow its own capabilities over time.

## How it works

1. **Create an issue** → the agent processes your request and replies as a comment.
2. **Comment on the issue** → the agent resumes the same session with full prior context.
3. **Everything is committed** → sessions, memory, and any file changes are pushed after every turn.
4. **The agent can extend itself** → it can write new skills, update `AGENTS.md`, and commit them so future sessions are smarter.

The agent reacts with 👀 while working and removes it when done.

### Repo as storage

All state lives in the repo:

```
AGENTS.md                   # agent identity + behavioral instructions (auto-loaded)
memory.log                  # append-only log of facts across all sessions
state/
  user.md                   # user profile (name, preferences)
  issues/
    1.json                  # maps issue #1 -> its Copilot CLI session ID
  copilot/
    sessions/
      <id>/                 # full session context for issue #1
.github/
  skills/
    bootstrap/SKILL.md      # first-run identity bootstrap
    write-skill/SKILL.md    # meta-skill: how to create new skills
    remember/SKILL.md       # how to write to memory.log
    <anything>/SKILL.md     # skills the agent writes for itself over time
```

Since everything is in git, it survives across ephemeral runners and is fully version-controlled.

## Setup

1. **Fork this repo**
2. **Create a fine-grained PAT** - go to **Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens → Fine-grained tokens** and create a token with the **Copilot Requests** permission. (Requires an active GitHub Copilot subscription — available on all plans.)
3. **Add the PAT as a secret** - go to your fork's **Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions** and create a secret named `COPILOT_PAT`.
4. **Hatch the agent** - open an issue titled anything (e.g. "Hello") and add the **`hatch`** label. The agent will introduce itself, ask about you, and write its own identity into the repo.
5. **Use it** - every subsequent issue is a task or conversation. The agent remembers everything across sessions.

## Security

The workflow only responds to repository **owners, members, and collaborators**. Random users cannot trigger the agent on public repos.

If you plan to use gitclaw for anything private, **make the repo private**. Public repos mean your conversation history is visible to everyone, but get generous GitHub Actions usage.

## Configuration

Everything lives in `.github/workflows/agent.yml` — no separate scripts. Common tweaks:

- **Model:** Add `--model MODEL` to the `copilot` invocation in the **Run agent** step (e.g. `--model claude-sonnet-4-5`).
- **Tools:** Restrict with `--available-tools read,grep,glob` for read-only analysis.
- **Reasoning:** Add `--experimental` and set `reasoning_effort` in `.copilot/settings.json`.
- **Trigger:** Adjust the `on:` block to filter by labels, assignees, etc.
- **AGENTS.md:** Already loaded automatically as custom instructions for the agent.

## Acknowledgments

Originally built on top of [pi-mono](https://github.com/badlogic/pi-mono) by [Mario Zechner](https://github.com/badlogic). Now powered by [GitHub Copilot CLI](https://docs.github.com/en/copilot/how-tos/copilot-cli).

Thanks to [ymichael](https://github.com/ymichael) for nerdsniping me with the idea of an agent that runs in GitHub Actions.
<exited with exit code 0>

data.content:
Hey Marcus! Let me check the README and sort this out 🦃

A few name ideas while I work on the README update:

**My top pick: `copilotclaw`** — clean, keeps the claw DNA, signals what it is  
Runner-up: `clawpilot` — "the claw that pilots", fun reversal  
Dark horse: `issuepilot` — describes exactly how it works (issues → AI agent)  
Poetic option: `copilot-nest` — where your Copilot hatches and grows (vibes with the bootstrap/hatch concept)

Now for the README fix — removing the ymichael credit and updating the acknowledgments to reflect you forked and transformed this:

data.reasoningText:
OK, I have the full context. Marcus forked gitclaw and made it Copilot-aware. He wants to:
1. Remove the thanks to ymichael line (that was from the original creator, not him)
2. Maybe rename the project - he suggested "gotpilotclaw" but wants me to suggest a good name

Let me think of some good names for this project. The project is:
- A GitHub Actions-based AI assistant
- Powered by GitHub Copilot CLI
- Evolved from gitclaw
- The agent can extend itself, write skills, etc.
- "Copilot" is a key part of it

result.content: Skill "session-stats" loaded successfully. Follow the instructions in the skill context.
result.detailedContent:
Skill loaded successfully ✅

---
name: session-stats
description: Show model usage stats and CI run times. Auto-appended at end of every response as a stats footer. Also invoke explicitly when user asks about usage, cost, run times, or stats.
allowed-tools: ["shell(bash:*)", "shell(gh:*)"]
---

# Session Stats Footer

Append this footer to **every response** in the session. Keep it compact — one block at the bottom.

## Format

```
---
📊 **Session** | premium: N calls (models) | free: N | standard: N | 🎟️ **Copilot**: 47/300 (15%) | 🏃 **CI**: ✅ 14:09 3m9s · ✅ 14:05 44s
```

## How to populate it

### Model usage (this session)
Track calls in the SQL session DB (`model_calls` table). Before each sub-agent call, insert a row. Then at response time, query counts:

```sql
SELECT tier, COUNT(*) as n, GROUP_CONCAT(DISTINCT model) as models
FROM model_calls GROUP BY tier;
```

If the table is empty (session just started), show `0 calls` for each tier.

### Copilot premium request quota
Run the bundled script — reads GitHub billing API using `BILLING_PAT` env var:

```bash
bash .github/skills/session-stats/scripts/premium-usage.sh schuerstedt
```

Output: `47 / 300 requests (15%)` or `unavailable` or `no BILLING_PAT — see AGENTS.md setup`

**Setup required**: `COPILOT_PAT` must have "Plan" user permission (read) — see AGENTS.md.

### CI run times
Run the bundled script (last 3 runs):

```bash
bash .github/skills/session-stats/scripts/ci-stats.sh 3
```

Condense to inline: `✅ 14:09 3m9s · ✅ 14:05 44s · ❌ 13:52 18s`

## Model tier reference

| Tier | Models |
|------|--------|
| free | `gpt-4.1`, `gpt-5-mini`, `gpt-5.1-codex-mini`, `claude-haiku-4.5` |
| standard | `claude-sonnet-4.5`, `gpt-5.1-codex`, `gpt-5.2-codex`, `gpt-5.3-codex` |
| premium | `claude-sonnet-4.6`, `claude-opus-4.5`, `claude-opus-4.6` |

## Tracking rule

Before every `task` tool call, insert into `model_calls`:
```sql
INSERT INTO model_calls (model, tier) VALUES ('gpt-4.1', 'free');
```

Count me (Claude Sonnet 4.6) as **1 premium call per user turn** — insert on first tool use each turn.

### Copilot premium request quota
Run the bundled script — reads GitHub billing API using `BILLING_PAT` env var:

```bash
bash .github/skills/session-stats/scripts/premium-usage.sh schuerstedt
```

Output: `47 / 300 requests (15%)` or `unavailable` or `no BILLING_PAT — see AGENTS.md setup`

**Setup required**: `COPILOT_PAT` must have "Plan" user permission (read) — see AGENTS.md.

### CI run times
Run the bundled script (last 3 runs):

```bash
bash .github/skills/session-stats/scripts/ci-stats.sh 3
```

| Tier | Models |
|------|--------|
| free | `gpt-4.1`, `gpt-5-mini`, `gpt-5.1-codex-mini`, `claude-haiku-4.5` |
| standard | `claude-sonnet-4.5`, `gpt-5.1-codex`, `gpt-5.2-codex`, `gpt-5.3-codex` |
| premium | `claude-sonnet-4.6`, `claude-opus-4.5`, `claude-opus-4.6` |

## Tracking rule

Before every `task` tool call, insert into `model_calls`:
```sql
INSERT INTO model_calls (model, tier) VALUES ('gpt-4.1', 'free');
```

Count me (Claude Sonnet 4.6) as **1 premium call per user turn** — insert on first tool use each turn.

data.reasoningText:
Marcus is asking about renaming the GitHub repo and whether he'd need a new secret token. Let me answer this from knowledge - GitHub repo renaming is straightforward and doesn't require new tokens.

Key facts:
1. Yes, you can rename a GitHub repo - go to Settings → General → Repository name
2. GitHub automatically creates redirects from the old URL to the new one
3. The COPILOT_PAT secret is a Personal Access Token - it's tied to the USER, not the repo name. So no, you don't need a new token.
4. The local git remote would need updating though (or it auto-redirects)

Let me be concise and helpful.

data.content:
Yep, dead simple — **Settings → General → Repository name** → type the new name → rename.

**On the token:** No new token needed. The `COPILOT_PAT` secret is a fine-grained PAT scoped to your *account* (Copilot permission), not tied to the repo name. It'll keep working after the rename with zero changes.

Two minor things to be aware of:
- GitHub creates **automatic redirects** from the old URL, so existing links/clones won't break
- If you have the repo cloned locally, you'd update the remote: `git remote set-url origin https://github.com/schuerstedt/NEW-NAME.git` — but the Actions runner re-clones fresh every run so that's already fine

That's it. Pick a name, rename, done. 🦃

---

result.content:
1. ![gitclaw banner](banner.jpeg)
2. 
3. A personal AI assistant that runs entirely through GitHub Issues and Actions. Like [OpenClaw](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw), but no servers or extra infrastructure.
4. 
5. Powered by [GitHub Copilot CLI](https://docs.github.com/en/copilot/how-tos/copilot-cli). Every issue becomes a chat thread with an AI agent — but more than that, the agent can **write new skills for itself**, update its own instructions, and commit them back to the repo. Future sessions inherit everything it commits. The assistant evolves as you use it.
6. 
7. Conversation history is committed to git, giving the agent long-term memory across sessions. It maintains a `memory.log`, a user profile, and can grow its own capabilities over time.
8. 
9. ## How it works
10. 
11. 1. **Create an issue** → the agent processes your request and replies as a comment.
12. 2. **Comment on the issue** → the agent resumes the same session with full prior context.
13. 3. **Everything is committed** → sessions, memory, and any file changes are pushed after every turn.
14. 4. **The agent can extend itself** → it can write new skills, update `AGENTS.md`, and commit them so future sessions are smarter.
15. 
16. The agent reacts with 👀 while working and removes it when done.
17. 
18. ### Repo as storage
19. 
20. All state lives in the repo:
21. 
22. ```
23. AGENTS.md                   # agent identity + behavioral instructions (auto-loaded)
24. memory.log                  # append-only log of facts across all sessions
25. state/
26.   user.md                   # user profile (name, preferences)
27.   issues/
28.     1.json                  # maps issue #1 -> its Copilot CLI session ID
29.   copilot/
30.     sessions/
31.       <id>/                 # full session context for issue #1
32. .github/
33.   skills/
34.     bootstrap/SKILL.md      # first-run identity bootstrap
35.     write-skill/SKILL.md    # meta-skill: how to create new skills
36.     remember/SKILL.md       # how to write to memory.log
37.     <anything>/SKILL.md     # skills the agent writes for itself over time
38. ```
39. 
40. Since everything is in git, it survives across ephemeral runners and is fully version-controlled.
41. 
42. ## Setup
43. 
44. 1. **Fork this repo**
45. 2. **Create a fine-grained PAT** - go to **Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens → Fine-grained tokens** and create a token with the **Copilot Requests** permission. (Requires an active GitHub Copilot subscription — available on all plans.)
46. 3. **Add the PAT as a secret** - go to your fork's **Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions** and create a secret named `COPILOT_PAT`.
47. 4. **Hatch the agent** - open an issue titled anything (e.g. "Hello") and add the **`hatch`** label. The agent will introduce itself, ask about you, and write its own identity into the repo.
48. 5. **Use it** - every subsequent issue is a task or conversation. The agent remembers everything across sessions.
49. 
50. ## Security
51. 
52. The workflow only responds to repository **owners, members, and collaborators**. Random users cannot trigger the agent on public repos.
53. 
54. If you plan to use gitclaw for anything private, **make the repo private**. Public repos mean your conversation history is visible to everyone, but get generous GitHub Actions usage.
55. 
56. ## Configuration
57. 
58. Everything lives in `.github/workflows/agent.yml` — no separate scripts. Common tweaks:
59. 
60. - **Model:** Add `--model MODEL` to the `copilot` invocation in the **Run agent** step (e.g. `--model claude-sonnet-4-5`).
61. - **Tools:** Restrict with `--available-tools read,grep,glob` for read-only analysis.
62. - **Reasoning:** Add `--experimental` and set `reasoning_effort` in `.copilot/settings.json`.
63. - **Trigger:** Adjust the `on:` block to filter by labels, assignees, etc.
64. - **AGENTS.md:** Already loaded automatically as custom instructions for the agent.
65. 
66. ## Acknowledgments
67. 
68. Forked from [gitclaw](https://github.com/schuerstedt/gitclaw) and rebuilt as a Copilot-native agent. Original project built on top of [pi-mono](https://github.com/badlogic/pi-mono) by [Mario Zechner](https://github.com/badlogic). Now powered by [GitHub Copilot CLI](https://docs.github.com/en/copilot/how-tos/copilot-cli).
69.

diff --git a/home/runner/work/copilotclaw/copilotclaw/README.md b/home/runner/work/copilotclaw/copilotclaw/README.md
index 0000000..0000000 100644
--- a/home/runner/work/copilotclaw/copilotclaw/README.md
+++ b/home/runner/work/copilotclaw/copilotclaw/README.md
@@ -1,69 +1,69 @@
 ![gitclaw banner](banner.jpeg)
 
 A personal AI assistant that runs entirely through GitHub Issues and Actions. Like [OpenClaw](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw), but no servers or extra infrastructure.
 
 Powered by [GitHub Copilot CLI](https://docs.github.com/en/copilot/how-tos/copilot-cli). Every issue becomes a chat thread with an AI agent — but more than that, the agent can **write new skills for itself**, update its own instructions, and commit them back to the repo. Future sessions inherit everything it commits. The assistant evolves as you use it.
 
 Conversation history is committed to git, giving the agent long-term memory across sessions. It maintains a `memory.log`, a user profile, and can grow its own capabilities over time.
 
 ## How it works
 
 1. **Create an issue** → the agent processes your request and replies as a comment.
 2. **Comment on the issue** → the agent resumes the same session with full prior context.
 3. **Everything is committed** → sessions, memory, and any file changes are pushed after every turn.
 4. **The agent can extend itself** → it can write new skills, update `AGENTS.md`, and commit them so future sessions are smarter.
 
 The agent reacts with 👀 while working and removes it when done.
 
 ### Repo as storage
 
 All state lives in the repo:
 
 ```
 AGENTS.md                   # agent identity + behavioral instructions (auto-loaded)
 memory.log                  # append-only log of facts across all sessions
 state/
   user.md                   # user profile (name, preferences)
   issues/
     1.json                  # maps issue #1 -> its Copilot CLI session ID
   copilot/
     sessions/
       <id>/                 # full session context for issue #1
 .github/
   skills/
     bootstrap/SKILL.md      # first-run identity bootstrap
     write-skill/SKILL.md    # meta-skill: how to create new skills
     remember/SKILL.md       # how to write to memory.log
     <anything>/SKILL.md     # skills the agent writes for itself over time
 ```
 
 Since everything is in git, it survives across ephemeral runners and is fully version-controlled.
 
 ## Setup
 
 1. **Fork this repo**
 2. **Create a fine-grained PAT** - go to **Settings → Developer settings → Personal access tokens → Fine-grained tokens** and create a token with the **Copilot Requests** permission. (Requires an active GitHub Copilot subscription — available on all plans.)
 3. **Add the PAT as a secret** - go to your fork's **Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions** and create a secret named `COPILOT_PAT`.
 4. **Hatch the agent** - open an issue titled anything (e.g. "Hello") and add the **`hatch`** label. The agent will introduce itself, ask about you, and write its own identity into the repo.
 5. **Use it** - every subsequent issue is a task or conversation. The agent remembers everything across sessions.
 
 ## Security
 
 The workflow only responds to repository **owners, members, and collaborators**. Random users cannot trigger the agent on public repos.
 
 If you plan to use gitclaw for anything private, **make the repo private**. Public repos mean your conversation history is visible to everyone, but get generous GitHub Actions usage.
 
 ## Configuration
 
 Everything lives in `.github/workflows/agent.yml` — no separate scripts. Common tweaks:
 
 - **Model:** Add `--model MODEL` to the `copilot` invocation in the **Run agent** step (e.g. `--model claude-sonnet-4-5`).
 - **Tools:** Restrict with `--available-tools read,grep,glob` for read-only analysis.
 - **Reasoning:** Add `--experimental` and set `reasoning_effort` in `.copilot/settings.json`.
 - **Trigger:** Adjust the `on:` block to filter by labels, assignees, etc.
 - **AGENTS.md:** Already loaded automatically as custom instructions for the agent.
 
 ## Acknowledgments
 
 Forked from [gitclaw](https://github.com/schuerstedt/gitclaw) and rebuilt as a Copilot-native agent. Original project built on top of [pi-mono](https://github.com/badlogic/pi-mono) by [Mario Zechner](https://github.com/badlogic). Now powered by [GitHub Copilot CLI](https://docs.github.com/en/copilot/how-tos/copilot-cli).
